front: Work: a priest and barber burn the books in this character's library, sparing La Galatea
Work: the priest praises Tirant lo Blanch, a Catalan romance, as 'the best book in the world' during this scene
back: Don Quixote — burning of the library (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: Samson Carrasco disguises himself as the Knight of the White Moon to defeat this character
Work: this character thinks a barber's basin full of curds is the helmet of Mambrino
Work: at an inn, the protagonist's friend is thrown around in a blanket and a hunchbacked servant crawls into bed with the protagonist
Work: the interpolated tale 'The Impertinently Curious Man' concerns Anselmo, Lothario, and the housewife Camilla (compared to a diamond and an ermine)
Work: in Part 2, Alvaro Tarfe signs an affidavit that an unauthorized sequel's characters are impostors
Work: the preface includes a story about a madman who inflates a dog with a pointed reed
Work: Pierre Menard recreates this novel word-for-word in a Borges story
Work: Miguel de Unamuno rebuked this novel's author for mistreating its protagonist; imagined a gravestone erected by Samson Carrasco
Work: Irving Leonard's Books of the Brave follows a shipment of first editions of this work crossing the Atlantic
Work: the dogs Cipion and Berganza converse by a hospital window, overheard by the syphilitic Campuzano; Freud signed 55 letters as 'Cipion'
back: The Colloquy of the Dogs — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: Berganza tells of Nicholas Snub-Nose's slaughterhouse; the witch Canizares says Berganza is the son of the witch Montiela
back: The Colloquy of the Dogs — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: Tomas Rodaja eats a spiked quince (love potion) and believes he is made of glass
back: The Lawyer of Glass — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: the glass-deluded character calls a hornet a 'slanderer' and escapes celebrity by moving to Flanders
back: The Lawyer of Glass — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: Campuzano is robbed by his wife Dona Estefania and overhears the dogs' conversation from his hospital bed
back: The Deceitful Marriage — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: two vagrant boys enter a confraternity of thieves led by Senor Monopodio in Seville
back: Rinconete y Cortadillo — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: a seventy-year-old man marries a fifteen-year-old girl and dies of jealousy
back: The Jealous Extremaduran — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: a rape victim uses a silver crucifix to prove her identity after her child is injured at a horse race
back: The Power of Blood — Exemplary Novels (Miguel de Cervantes)

front: Work: pastoral novel featuring the shepherdess Galatea loved by Elicio and Erastro, set near the Tagus river; imitated Montemayor's Diana
Work: this first novel by Cervantes is spared during the burning of Don Quixote's library, judged to have 'some good invention'
Work: features a journey to the wedding of Daranio and Silveria
Work: satirical poem about poetry; Lope de Vega is rained out of a cloud onto a boat of poets; Gongora is praised
Work: first act ends with a discussion between Spain and the river Duero after a speech by Scipio Africanus; the sorcerer Marquino resurrects a corpse
back: The Siege of Numantia (Miguel de Cervantes)
work: Plays (The Siege of Numantia and others)

front: Work: a divorce court judge listens to four oral arguments, then refuses to let anybody divorce
work: Plays (The Siege of Numantia and others)

front: Author: an annual prize for Spanish-language literature is named after this author
Author: this writer was a veteran of the Battle of Lepanto and was imprisoned in Algiers
Author: excommunicated multiple times
Author: nicknamed Lope de Vega 'The Phoenix of Wits' and parodied him as the puppeteer Gines de Pasamonte
Author: The Travels of Persiles and Sigismunda (posthumous): Rutilio describes men turning into wolves; Carpentier cites this as the 'marvelous real'
